---
title: "Response to comments: seqwrap: an R package for flexible iterative fitting of high-dimensional data"
author:
  - name: Chidimma Echebiri
    email: chidimma.echebiri@inn.no
    affiliation:
      - name: University of Inland Norway
        department: Department of Biotechnology, PhD programme in applied ecology and biotechnology
  - name: Rafi Ahmad
    email: rafi.ahmad@inn.no
    affiliation: 
      - name: University of Inland Norway
        department: Department of Biotechnology
      - name: UiT - The Arctic University of Norway, Tromsø, Norway
        department: Institute of Clinical Medicine, Faculty of Health Sciences
  - name: Stian Ellefsen
    email: stian.ellefsen@inn.no
    affiliation: 
      - name: University of Inland Norway
        department: Department of Public Health and Sport Sciences 
  - name: Daniel Hammarström*
    email: daniel.hammarstrom@inn.no
    affiliation: 
      - name: University of Inland Norway
        department: Department of Public Health and Sport Sciences 
    attributes:
      corresponding: true
format: typst
csl: resources/bmc-bioinformatics.csl
---

`#v(3em)`{=typst}

### Editor's comments and authors' responses
`#strong([Authors response.])`{=typst} 
Dear Editor,
We thank you for your detailed feedback and the opportunity to further revise our manuscript. We appreciate the time and effort invested in evaluating our manuscript. We understand the importance of reproducibility in research, more so in a methodological paper as we have written. 

We are addressing your comments as follows
1. Complete R code/scripts used to generate all analyses, figures, and tables reported in the manuscript. 

`#strong([Authors response.])`{=typst} Complete R codes and scripts used to generate the results presented in the manuscript have been published in the github repository  https://github.com/trainome/seqwrap-paper with a detailed the README section containing details about the specific files to reproduce specific results or figures in the manuscript.


2. All datasets required to reproduce the results, or clear instructions for accessing them if they are publicly available

`#strong([Authors response.])`{=typst}The case study data used in the manuscript and the supplementary document are are openly available ; Pillon et al ., [@pillon2022], GSE202295; and  Dungan et al. [@dungan2022], GSE195707 respectively.   The simulation data sets can be generated using the codes specified in the repository. However, the simulated data can also be downloaded from DataverseNO: Chidimma Echebiri, Stian Ellefsen, Rafi Ahmad, and Daniel Hammarstr&ouml;m. 2026. “Simulated Data Sets for: Seqwrap: An R Package for Flexible Iterative Fitting of High-Dimensional Data.” DataverseNO. https://doi.org/doi:10.18710/I7U71O.


3. The code should be provided in an executable and well-documented form, allowing readers to reproduce the results step by step.

`#strong([Authors response.])`{=typst} The codes in each file are executable and well commented. The README section of the repository https://github.com/trainome/seqwrap-paper gives step-by-step direction on how to reproduce the results


4. If the datasets and scripts are already included within the R package or repository, please ensure that this is clearly indicated in the manuscript and provide direct links and instructions for reproducing the results.

`#strong([Authors response.])`{=typst} We have updated the *Availability of data and materials* section of the manuscript which now reads 
  All data presented in the paper (and supplementary material) are openly available (Pillon et al ., [@pillon2022], GSE202295; Dungan et al. [@dungan2022], GSE195707). The code for the simulation study is available at https://github.com/trainome/seqwrap-paper. Simulation results can be replicated using code provided in the repository https://github.com/trainome/seqwrap-paper, simulation data sets and results can also be downloaded from DataverseNO: Chidimma Echebiri, Stian Ellefsen, Rafi Ahmad, and Daniel Hammarstr&ouml;m. 2026. “Simulated Data Sets for: Seqwrap: An R Package for Flexible Iterative Fitting of High-Dimensional Data.” DataverseNO. https://doi.org/doi:10.18710/I7U71O.
  
  
### Note to Editor

In the first review round, Reviewer 1 recommended we expand our literature review and include three specific references (Chen and Qiu 2023, DOI: 10.32614/RJ‑2023‑080; Chen 2022, DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0276664; and Chen and Tsao 2024, DOI: 10.1093/bioinformatics/btae731). We found all three references relevant to the broader methodological landscape.  

However, because our manuscript focuses specifically on R‑based packages, we decided to only include the two references that introduce R‑based tools. The remaining reference (Chen 2022, DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0276664) describes a Python‑based workflow. For reasons of conceptual and technical consistency in the manuscript, and to avoid mixing software ecosystems in a way that might distract from the paper's scope, we chose not to incorporate that reference. 

In the current review round, the Reviewer 1 again encouraged us to add the previously excluded reference. After discussion among the authors and reconsidering the scope of the manuscript, we believe that including a Python‑based tool in a paper focused on R packages would introduce unnecessary conceptual heterogeneity. For this reason, we respectfully maintain our earlier decision not to add this reference.

We hope this explanation clarifies our rationale, and we remain open to further guidance should the editorial team feel that including the reference is essential for our paper. 



### Reviewer comments and authors' responses

`#strong([Authors response.])`{=typst} We thank the reviewers for once again reviewing our manuscript and making comments and recommendations all aimed at upholding the standard expectation for methodological papers and software packages. We are addressing the reviewers' comments below.

#### Reviewer 1

I appreciate the authors' great effort in preparing this revision. The authors have adequately addressed most of the previous comments. However, I have two additional very minor comments and recommend minor revision:

1.   I just noticed that a suggested reference (Chen 2022, DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0276664) appears to be missing from the revised manuscript. I would suggest adding this reference, as it also developed an R package for high-dimensional data, particularly for classification problems.

`#strong([Authors response.])`{=typst} We thank the reviewer for the suggestion. We agree that the suggested reference (Chen 2022, DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0276664) is relevant in the context of high-dimensional data analysis tools. However, we note that the reference is a Python-based tool, whereas our manuscript focuses on R-based methods intended for R-based analysis. To maintain conceptual and technical consistency, we included only the R-based packages recommended by the reviewer which helped not just position our package but made the review more comprehensive.

2.  Since the manuscript title contains the term “high-dimensional,” users or readers may naturally expect some form of variable selection. This is why I raised this comment previously.
    According to your response, you mentioned that “each target gets its own set of estimates, and selection between targets as features has to be done by the user after model fitting.” Does this imply that informative genes should be pre-determined by the users? I would suggest that the authors clarify this point in the manuscript

`#strong([Authors response.])`{=typst} We thank the reviewer for pointing out the potential for misunderstanding regarding variable selection. To clarify, our package is not designed to perform formal variable selection across predictors. Instead, the package fits a separate model for each target (e.g., gene or protein), and the resulting estimates are returned to the user. This does not require users to pre‑select informative targets; rather, targets can be evaluated and filtered after model fitting based on their estimated effects.

We have revised the manuscript to make this explicit. The revised text now appears in .....................................

#### Reviewer 2

Many thanks for considering my previous comments. However, I believe that in order to have a fully reproducible paper, an associated file with the R code to run all the necessary analyses and plots in paper is needed. The authors have provided a supplementary.pdf file with lots of R code chunks. However, this is not suitable for reproducibility as it involves copying and pasting tens of R code chunks.

Hence, please provide a single file with R code so that we can reproduce all the examples in the paper. If simulations are used, please use set.seed() before sampling the simulated data. Furthermore, make sure that you comment the R code appropriately so that the different results and plots in the paper can be identified in the code

`#strong([Authors response.])`{=typst} We thank the reviewer for this suggestion regarding reproducibility. 
We agree that providing code in a fully executable form is essential for transparent and reproducible research. In response, we have published the dedicated GitHub repository for the manuscript (https://github.com/trainome/seqwrap-paper) which now contains

1.  A single, fully executable Quarto (.qmd) file that contains all the R code chunks to reproduce the analysis in the supplementary document. This allows running the entire workflow from start to finish and avoids copying and pasting code.
2.  A single, fully executable R script that contains all the codes necessary to reproduce the analysis and figures in the paper. Wherever simulated data are generated, we set seed values using `set.seed(1)` to ensure exact reproducibility.
3.  A detailed README section that explains step‑by‑step how to reproduce each result, figure, and table from the manuscript, including references to section numbers and figure numbers

Because the simulations used in the paper generate very large data objects, rerunning the simulation from scratch is computationally expensive. To address this while still ensuring full reproducibility:

-   We have deposited the simulation output dataset on DataverseNO: Chidimma Echebiri, Stian Ellefsen, Rafi Ahmad, and Daniel Hammarstr&ouml;m. 2026. “Simulated Data Sets for: Seqwrap: An R Package for Flexible Iterative Fitting of High-Dimensional Data.” DataverseNO. https://doi.org/doi:10.18710/I7U71O.

-   For data simulation in the provided R script , an optional flag that automatically downloads the dataset from Dataverse instead of re‑running the entire simulation has been provided. Users who prefer to regenerate the data may do so by setting this flag accordingly; all random processes use `set.seed(1)` to ensure reproducibility.

We have also added comments throughout the R script so users can easily identify which code produces each figure and result in the manuscript.

We believe these additions fully address the reviewer’s helpful suggestions and significantly enhance the reproducibility and transparency of the work.
